content.text: is the practice of storing the output of an LLM call (or any expensive computation) so that an identical or sufficiently similar future request can be served from storage instead of invoking the model again. In agentic systems, where an agent may call a model hundreds of times per session — and many of those calls share the same system prompt, tool definitions, or factual lookups — caching can cut both latency and cost by orders of magnitude.
Caching operates at two complementary levels:
(your code stores and retrieves full responses keyed on the prompt) and
(the LLM provider reuses a KV-cache of the token prefix, reducing token processing cost on their side). Both are complementary and should usually be used together.

Agentic workflows amplify the cost and latency of LLM calls. A research agent that calls Claude five times to analyze the same background context has paid for that context five times over. A customer-support bot that includes a 10,000-token product manual in every system prompt spends the same prefill cost on every turn. Response caching removes that repetition.

LLM API calls are priced per token. Caching eliminates redundant token processing. Anthropic's prompt caching charges cached input tokens at 10% of the standard input rate, cutting prefill costs by 90%.
A cache hit returns in milliseconds versus the 1–30 seconds a full LLM call takes. For multi-turn agents, this compounds across every step.
LLM providers impose rate limits (tokens per minute, requests per minute). Cache hits bypass these limits entirely, letting you serve more requests without hitting quotas.

Cached responses are deterministic. For tasks where repeatability matters (data extraction, classification), a cache hit guarantees the same output — eliminating temperature-induced variance on repeated calls.
Immediate cost savings on repeated prompts:
Any task that re-uses the same system prompt or context block benefits instantly. Prompt caching on Claude reduces prefill cost to 10% of the normal rate.
Cache hits under 1 ms make agents feel instantaneous to end-users, even when the underlying model call would have taken several seconds.
Hitting the cache does not consume provider tokens-per-minute quota, effectively multiplying your throughput capacity for repeated queries.
If the LLM provider is temporarily unavailable or slow, serving a cached response keeps the agent pipeline alive for known-good prompts.
Recording and replaying cached responses enables deterministic testing without live API access, cutting test suite cost and flakiness.
Cached responses reflect the world at cache-fill time. Facts change, tools update, and a stale response can mislead an agent. TTL tuning and cache invalidation logic are required.

If prompts are highly variable (e.g., contain user-specific data, timestamps, or random seeds), few prompts will match and the hit rate will be near zero. Exact-match caching only helps when prompts are truly repeated.
LLM responses can be kilobytes to megabytes. A large cache requires proportional storage and eviction policies; uncapped caches leak memory.

Fuzzy/semantic caching (matching on embedding similarity) may return a response to a question that is similar but not identical. For factual or structured-output tasks, a wrong-but-close cache hit is worse than a miss.

Provider-side prompt caching has a one-time write surcharge (1.25× standard input rate on Anthropic) when the cache is populated. The savings only break even after the cached prefix is reused enough times.
Storing LLM responses in a shared cache may expose one user's data to another if the cache key is constructed carelessly. Key isolation per user is mandatory for multi-tenant agents.
The core flow for application-side response caching is:
normalize the model name, sort any unordered parameters, and serialize to a stable string. A nondeterministic serialization produces different keys for equivalent requests and kills hit rate.
apply SHA-256 (or similar) to the canonical string. The hash is short, constant-length, and safe for use as a Redis key or filename.
look up the key. On a hit, deserialize and return the stored response. On a miss, proceed.
execute the API call and receive the response.
write the response to the cache under the computed key with an appropriate time-to-live. Ephemeral facts get short TTLs (minutes); stable knowledge can use hours or days.
hand the caller the live response. Subsequent identical requests will hit the cache.

breakpoints) works differently: the provider caches the KV activations of the prefix up to a marked checkpoint. No application-level key management is needed — the provider tracks it automatically based on the token content and the
annotation. The caller only pays 10% of the normal input token rate when the cached prefix is reused.

attrs.alt: Response caching architecture: Agent sends request to Cache Layer, which checks for a HIT (returns cached response) or MISS (forwards to LLM Provider and stores result). Cache backends include Redis, in-memory, semantic, prompt caching, and CDN.

content.text: The diagram shows two caching tiers working together. The
(application-side) intercepts every request before it reaches the provider. A cache HIT returns in milliseconds at zero token cost. A cache MISS flows to the
can still reduce cost by reusing the cached KV state of the system prompt prefix. The response is then stored in the application cache for future identical requests.
any agentic system with a long, stable system prompt (tool definitions, personas, large knowledge bases) benefits from provider-side prompt caching on every call.
a finite set of common questions gets asked repeatedly. Application-side caching with exact-match keys on the normalized question string can serve the majority of traffic from cache.
when running the same extraction or classification prompt against many documents, prompt caching on the instruction prefix saves 90% of prefill cost per document.
when a supervisor and multiple sub-agents all use the same background context, each agent benefits independently from the same cached prefix.

prompts are unique per request (personalized generation, creative writing with random seeds), the response must always reflect live data (stock prices, real-time alerts), or the task requires fresh model output for safety reasons (medical triage, security decisions).
Redis for distributed/persistent caching, a Python
for single-process testing, or a semantic cache (GPTCache, LangChain's
Define a stable cache key function:

serialize model name, temperature, max_tokens, system prompt, and all user messages to a canonical JSON string, then SHA-256 hash it. Exclude fields that are not part of the output (e.g., request IDs, timestamps).
Wrap the LLM call in a caching decorator or class:
check cache before calling, store on miss. Keep this layer thin so it is easy to audit and bypass when needed.
annotate the stable prefix of your system prompt with
in the Anthropic Messages API. The breakpoint must be at or after the 1,024-token mark.
short TTLs for dynamic data (minutes), long TTLs for stable knowledge (hours or days), infinite TTL (no expiry) only for immutable content like code generation from a fixed spec.
prefix every cache key with a user ID or tenant ID to prevent cross-user data leakage in multi-tenant systems.
emit cache hit, miss, and error counters to your metrics system. A hit rate below 20% suggests key construction is too specific or prompts are too variable.

import hashlib
import json
import redis
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
cache = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)
CACHE_TTL = 3600  # 1 hour


def make_cache_key(model: str, messages: list, system: str, **kwargs) -> str:
    """Produce a stable SHA-256 key from the request parameters."""
    payload = json.dumps(
        {
            "model": model,
            "system": system,
            "messages": messages,
            # Include any deterministic kwargs (e.g. max_tokens, temperature)
            **{k: v for k, v in sorted(kwargs.items())},
        },
        sort_keys=True,
    )
    return "llm:" + hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()


def cached_completion(
    model: str,
    messages: list,
    system: str = "",
    ttl: int = CACHE_TTL,
    **kwargs,
) -> str:
    key = make_cache_key(model, messages, system, **kwargs)

    # Check cache
    cached = cache.get(key)
    if cached:
        print("[cache HIT]")
        return json.loads(cached)

    # Cache miss — call the model
    print("[cache MISS]")
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        system=system,
        messages=messages,
        max_tokens=kwargs.get("max_tokens", 1024),
    )
    result = response.content[0].text

    # Store with TTL
    cache.setex(key, ttl, json.dumps(result))
    return result


# Usage
answer = cached_completion(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    system="You are a concise assistant.",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}],
    max_tokens=64,
)
print(answer)
Provider-side prompt caching with Anthropic cache_control

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Large, stable system prompt (must be >= 1,024 tokens to be eligible)
PRODUCT_MANUAL = """
... (10,000 tokens of product documentation) ...
"""

def answer_support_question(user_question: str) -> str:
    """
    Each call reuses the cached KV-state of the product manual prefix.
    Only the user question adds new uncached tokens.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": PRODUCT_MANUAL,
                # Mark the end of the stable prefix as a cache breakpoint.
                # Anthropic caches all tokens up to and including this block.
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
            }
        ],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_question}],
    )

    # Inspect cache usage in response metadata
    usage = response.usage
    print(f"Input tokens:        {usage.input_tokens}")
    print(f"Cache creation:      {getattr(usage, 'cache_creation_input_tokens', 0)}")
    print(f"Cache read (saved):  {getattr(usage, 'cache_read_input_tokens', 0)}")
    # Cache read tokens cost 10% of normal input rate.

    return response.content[0].text


# First call: cache miss — prefix is stored (1.25x write surcharge)
r1 = answer_support_question("How do I reset my password?")

# Second call: cache hit — prefix reused at 10% cost
r2 = answer_support_question("What are the system requirements?")

A legal-document review agent processes 500 contracts per day. Each contract is sent to Claude with the same 8,000-token system prompt containing legal guidelines, output schemas, and examples. Without caching, every call pays full prefill cost for those 8,000 tokens. With prompt caching enabled, only the first call per 5-minute cache window pays the write surcharge; all subsequent calls read the cached prefix at 10% cost. The agent also caches complete responses for any document hash it has seen before, so re-runs during QA do not cost anything.

import hashlib
import json
import redis
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
cache = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)

LEGAL_GUIDELINES = """
You are a legal document review assistant.
... (8,000 tokens of guidelines, schemas, and examples) ...
"""


def review_contract(contract_text: str) -> dict:
    """
    Two-level caching:
    1. Application-level: full response cached by document hash.
    2. Provider-level: system prompt prefix cached by Anthropic.
    """
    # Level 1: application cache keyed on document content hash
    doc_hash = hashlib.sha256(contract_text.encode()).hexdigest()
    cache_key = f"contract_review:{doc_hash}"

    cached = cache.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return json.loads(cached)

    # Level 2: provider-side prompt caching via cache_control
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": LEGAL_GUIDELINES,
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},  # cache the 8k prefix
            }
        ],
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": f"Review the following contract and return JSON:\n\n{contract_text}",
            }
        ],
    )

    result = json.loads(response.content[0].text)

    # Store full response for 24 hours — contracts don't change
    cache.setex(cache_key, 86400, json.dumps(result))

    usage = response.usage
    cache_read = getattr(usage, "cache_read_input_tokens", 0)
    print(
        f"Tokens — input: {usage.input_tokens}, "        f"cache_read: {cache_read}, output: {usage.output_tokens}"
    )
    return result


# Process 500 contracts
contracts = [load_contract(i) for i in range(500)]
reviews = [review_contract(c) for c in contracts]
# After the first call per 5-min window, each contract saves ~$0.024 in prefill tokens.
Including timestamps or request IDs in the cache key:
these are different on every call, making every request a cache miss. Only include parameters that actually affect model output.
extra whitespace, different Unicode normalizations, or non-deterministic key ordering in JSON produce different hashes for semantically identical prompts.
Placing the cache_control breakpoint too early:
Anthropic prompt caching requires at least 1,024 tokens before the breakpoint. Placing it at token 800 silently does nothing.
if the system prompt includes the user's name, account balance, or session-specific data, the cache key must include those fields — or the cache must be scoped per user.
Using a shared cache key namespace across users:
without per-user key prefixes, user A's cached response (which may contain private data) can be returned to user B with the same question.
Setting TTL to infinity for mutable data:
product prices, legal regulations, and tool schemas change. A cached response with no TTL will silently serve stale data indefinitely.
if Redis goes down and cache reads throw exceptions, the agent should fall through to a live LLM call, not crash. Always wrap cache reads in try/except and treat errors as misses.
Always use provider prompt caching for any system prompt over 1,024 tokens.
The write surcharge is recovered after just two reads. For high-volume agents, the savings are dramatic.
Keep the cached prefix stable and at the front.
The cache breakpoint invalidates whenever any token before it changes. Put all variable content (user messages, document snippets) after the breakpoint.
. This makes it easy to invalidate all keys for a given namespace or user.
live data (minutes), general knowledge (hours), static reference material (days). Never use a single global TTL.
Monitor hit rate and act on it:
a hit rate below 30% suggests caching is not well-matched to your traffic pattern. Consider whether semantic caching or a coarser key (stripping non-essential parameters) would help.
Implement cache warming for predictable traffic:
if you know the top 100 FAQ queries, pre-populate the cache during deployment. This eliminates cold-start latency spikes.
Use write-through or write-behind patterns for high-traffic keys:
if many requests for the same key arrive simultaneously (cache stampede), use a locking pattern (Redis
) to let only one request populate the cache while others wait.
Cache performance characteristics differ sharply between application-side and provider-side caching:

sub-millisecond latency, millions of reads per second per node. For agentic workloads at scale, Redis Cluster spreads load across shards. Memory usage scales with response size × number of unique cached prompts.

Anthropic reports up to 85% latency reduction on the prefill phase when the prompt cache is warm. For a 10,000-token system prompt, this saves the time to process ~9,000 tokens on the provider's GPUs.
add zero network overhead. Suitable for single-instance deployments or batch jobs. Not shared across processes or restarts.

embedding a query for semantic lookup takes 10–50 ms. The additional round-trip to a vector DB adds another 5–20 ms. This overhead is worth it only if the semantic hit rate is significantly higher than exact-match and the LLM call it replaces takes multiple seconds.

JSON serialization of large LLM responses (multi-page text, tool call payloads) can take several milliseconds. For sub-millisecond cache requirements, use MessagePack or store already-serialized bytes.
to retain the most recently used responses under memory pressure. For strict budget caps,
(least-frequently-used) preserves high-traffic keys over rarely-accessed ones.
Sub-ms reads, TTL, LRU/LFU eviction, cluster mode
Plug-in caching for LangChain LLM calls
Up to 25x less memory than Redis for same data
Pay-per-use; good for bursty agentic workloads
High on hit, overhead on miss
Tasks where quality tradeoff is acceptable
Key isolation per user is mandatory in multi-tenant systems.

Always prefix cache keys with a user or tenant identifier. Never construct keys from the prompt alone; a different user can submit the same question and receive a cached response that may contain the first user's data.

LLM responses that contain PII, medical, or financial information should be encrypted at rest in the cache. Redis supports encryption via TLS for data in transit; application-level encryption protects data at rest.

If an attacker can write to your Redis instance, they could inject malicious cached responses. Use Redis AUTH, ACLs, and network segmentation. Validate the schema of deserialized responses before using them.
Never let a cache failure prevent the agent from operating. Wrap all cache reads and writes in try/except and fall through to a live LLM call on any cache error. Log the error; do not crash.

When a model is updated, your system prompt changes, or underlying facts change, stale cache entries must be invalidated. Implement explicit cache-bust endpoints (or versioned key prefixes) so operators can clear targeted entries without flushing the entire cache.
Do not cache refusals or error responses.

Check the response for error codes or model refusal indicators before caching. A transient rate-limit error or a model refusal cached indefinitely would serve incorrect content to all future identical requests.

Response caching operates at two layers: application-side (full response stored by your code, served in milliseconds at zero token cost) and provider-side prompt caching (KV-cache of the token prefix, reducing prefill cost to 10% of normal).
Enable Anthropic prompt caching on any system prompt over 1,024 tokens. Annotate the stable prefix with
; the break-even on the 1.25× write surcharge is just two reads.
Build cache keys from canonical, sorted, deterministic serializations of only the parameters that affect model output. Exclude request IDs, timestamps, and metadata.
Namespace every cache key per user or tenant. A shared key namespace in a multi-tenant system is a data-leakage vulnerability.
Use TTLs matched to content volatility: minutes for live data, hours for general knowledge, days for stable reference material. Never use infinite TTL for mutable content.
Always fall through to a live LLM call on cache errors (cache-aside pattern). A cache is an optimization, not a hard dependency — the agent must function when the cache is unavailable.

Monitor hit rate. A rate below 20–30% indicates that prompt variability is too high for exact-match caching; consider semantic caching, prompt normalization, or restructuring prompts to move variable parts after a stable cached prefix.
Do not cache model refusals, error responses, or streaming-incomplete outputs. Validate the response structure before writing to cache.
